FROM:KONICA FAX                TO:3272026                DEC 12, 1990  6:20PM  P.01


                            EBER & NAKAGAWA
MARTIN H. EBER*                                                AREA CODE 415
GREGG* H. NAKAGAWA              ATTORNEYS AT LAW               TELEPHONE 362-2657

WILLIAM J. LIES             650 CALIFORNIA STREET
                            TWENTY-SIXTH FLOOR            TELECOPIER:(415)362-1021
*A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION
                            SAN FRANCISCO,CALIFORNIA 94108


                            FAX TRANSMITTAL


Please route to:    Jonathan Rothman, Esq.
                    Legal Department
                    FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICE COMMISSION




FAX Number      :   (916) 327-2026


From            :   Martin H. Eber, Esq.
                    EBER & NAKAGAWA

Date            :   December 12, 1990

Operator        :   The total number of pages being transmitted

                    herewith is    14     including this page.

Specific
Instructions    :   ___________________________


                    ___________________________


                    ___________________________


                    ___________________________


                    ___________________________


                    ___________________________



If you do not receive all pages, or any portions of the
transmittal are illegible, please call (415) 362-2657
immediately.

FROM:KONICA FAX                TO:3272025                DEC 12, 1992  5:25PM P.09


                           PATRICK E. MASON, Ph.D.
                            CONSULTING ECONOMIST
                           909 MONTGOMERY STREET
                              THIRD FLOOR
                         SAN FRANCISCO, CA 94133
                             (415) 788-1786


December 6, 1990


Martin H. Eber, Esq.
EBER & NAKAGAWA
650 California, 26th Floor
San Francisco, CA  94104

Dear Mr. Eber:

     I have been asked by you to render an opinion regarding the
potential price impact on existing rental housing resulting from
passage of the proposed exemption from the condominium conversion
ordinance (The Tenant Homeownership Opportunity Program of 1990).
Specifically, your question is: Would the conversion of 1000
rental units to condominiums under the rules proposed by this
amendment have any significant positive or negative effect on
rental rates for apartments remaining in the market?

     Ordinarily, a reduction in the supply of a type of housing
given the same level of demand would, a priori, put upward pres-
sure on rental rates. This is demonstrated in Chart 1 where a
downward shift in the supply schedule from S₁ to S₂ results in a
reduction of units from N₁ to N₂ and a higher price of apartments,
from R₁ to R₂.

     However, key provisions of the proposal are that there must
be participation by two-thirds of the renters in the particular
building, and those who do not wish to participate cannot be
evicted. In other words, the reduction of units will be equalled
by a simultaneous reduction in the number of renters.

     This constitutes simultaneous downward shifts of both the
supply and demand schedules. Under this condition, there is no
a priori reason to expect the price of rentals to go up or down.
In fact, the most probable scenario is that rental areas would
remain the same. Chart 2 demonstrates the impact of the simul-
taneous shift in demand and supply schedules on rental prices.
A reduction in units along with an equal reduction in renters,
cet. par., will leave the rental rates at the same level.

     In Charts 3 and 4, this is also demonstrated under the two
extrema assumptions of perfectly elastic and perfectly inelastic
demand conditions for rental housing. (In reality, we are always
somewhere between these extremes.)


<[illegible]>

FROM:KONICA FAX          TO:3272025          DEC 12, 1990    5:26PM  P.10


Martin H. Eber, Esq.
December 6, 1990
Page 2


     Charts 3 and 4 demonstrate that under the two extreme
conditions of demand elasticity, the results are the same --
indeterminate impact on rental rates.

     In conclusion, economic theory as applied to this proposed
amendment demonstrates that there can be no a priori assumption
relating to the direction, if any, or the magnitude of rental rate
changes. In fact, the most probable outcome is that there would
be no change in rent levels at all.


                                        Sincerely,


                                        Patrick A. Mason, Ph.D.
                                        Consulting Economist


PFM:pj

Eber, Esq., and the recipient is Jonathan Rothman, Esq., Legal Department of the Fair Political Practice Commission. The fax number for the recipient is (916) 327-2026. The date of transmission is December 12, 1990. The operator notes that the total number of pages being transmitted herewith is 14, including this page. There are no specific instructions provided. If you do not receive all pages, or any portions of the transmittal are illegible, please call (415) 362-2657 immediately. December 6, 1990 Martin H. Eber, Esq. EBER & NAKAGAWA 650 California, 26th Floor San Francisco, CA 94104 Dear Mr. Eber: I have been asked by you to render an opinion regarding the potential price impact on existing rental housing resulting from passage of the proposed exemption from the condominium conversion ordinance (The Tenant Homeownership Opportunity Program of 1990). Specifically, your question is: Would the conversion of 1000 rental units to condominiums under the rules proposed by this amendment have any significant positive or negative effect on rental rates for apartments remaining in the market? Ordinarily, a reduction in the supply of a type of housing given the same level of demand would, a priori, put upward pressure on rental rates. This is demonstrated in Chart 1 where a downward shift in the supply schedule from S₁ to S₂ results in a reduction of units from N₁ to N₂ and a higher price of apartments, from R₁ to R₂. However, key provisions of the proposal are that there must be participation by two-thirds of the renters in the particular building and those who do not wish to participate cannot be evicted. In other words, the reduction of units will be equalled by a simultaneous reduction in the number of renters. This constitutes simultaneous downward shifts of both the supply and demand schedules. Under this condition, there is no a priori reason to expect the price of rentals to go up or down. In fact, the most probable scenario is that rental rates would remain the same. Chart 2 demonstrates the impact of the simultaneous shift in demand and supply schedules on rental prices. A reduction in units along with an equal reduction in renters, cet. par., will leave the rental rates at the same level. In Charts 3 and 4, this is also demonstrated under the two extreme assumptions of perfectly elastic and perfectly inelastic demand conditions for rental housing. (In reality, we are always somewhere between these extremes.) Martin H. Eber, Esq.
